failure_modes.text: Escalation after the decision has already lost its best branch can keep effort flowing into a dead path.
failure_modes.source_quote: Escalation after the decision has already lost its best branch
failure_modes.mitigation: Define explicit exit criteria and reversal triggers before commitment so the team has pre-agreed conditions for changing course.

failure_modes.text: Entrenchment and inertia can make the team defend prior posture long after the evidence moved.
failure_modes.mitigation: Schedule regular re-evaluation checkpoints that force the team to justify continued investment from current evidence, not from prior momentum.

failure_modes.text: Self-justification can turn consistency pressure into a refusal to update strategy.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate the sunk cost from the forward decision: ask what the team would do if starting fresh with the same information today.

premortem_questions.text: What evidence would make us reverse course rather than defend the original commitment?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.note: The raw source uses the question to unpack assumptions; this chunk adapts it to reversal discipline.

premortem_questions.text: If this path is failing quietly, what would we notice first?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.note: Grounded in the raw downside-checking logic and escalation failure table.

premortem_questions.text: Are we protecting the goal, or just protecting the story that we were right to start?
premortem_questions.note: Conservative normalization from the raw model's consistency-pressure logic.

heuristics.text: Separate goal-setting, diagnosis, and design so commitment to one step does not pre-commit the next.
heuristics.source_quote: perform the steps one at a time and in order

heuristics.text: Use outside challenge to test whether the current path still deserves commitment.
heuristics.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.text: Treat small commitments as leverage carefully because they can ratchet identity and posture faster than evidence updates.

curation_notes.summary: The raw file has unusually strong escalation and self-justification content, so the chunk set centers on reversal discipline rather than generic caution.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor runtime-style triggers because this layer is about intervention semantics, not route activation.
open_questions: This source is richer on failure modes than on explicit pre-mortem phrasing, so some questions remain reviewed normalizations.

allies: A later relation wave could preserve the raw support links around outside view, implementation discipline, and structured challenge.
antagonists: The raw source is strong on escalation, sunk posture, and identity-driven rigidity as future antagonistic relation material.
structured_tensions: Commitment-bias likely has future structured-tension value against BATNA and route-correction models.
